items.question_text: Under 8-3-1, when one aircraft requires a different time-based longitudinal standard than another, apply the _____ of the two standards.
items.explanation: Per 8-3-1b, the larger of the two standards is applied.

choices.text: larger

items.question_text: Under 8-3-1, where in JO 7110.65 are distance-based longitudinal separation prescriptions found?
items.explanation: Per 8-3-1c, longitudinal separation expressed in distance may be applied as prescribed in Chapter 6, Nonradar.

choices.text: Chapter 5, Radar.
Chapter 6, Nonradar.
Chapter 4, IFR.
Chapter 7, Visual.

items.question_text: Under 8-3-1, if an updated control estimate shows spacing has dropped below the prescribed minimum, what two actions must the controller take?
items.explanation: Per 8-3-1d, controllers must ensure separation is reestablished and issue traffic information as necessary.

choices.text: Reestablish separation and wait for pilot reports before issuing any traffic information.
Convert to visual separation and advise the supervisor.
Ensure separation is reestablished and issue traffic information as necessary.
Continue using the original estimate because updated estimates are advisory only.

items.question_text: A trainee believes that when one aircraft needs 10-minute longitudinal separation and another needs 15-minute separation, you should split the difference and use 12-13 minutes. Is this correct under 8-3-1?
items.explanation: Per 8-3-1b, the larger standard must be applied — not an average or compromise value.

choices.text: Yes — averaging is acceptable as long as the result exceeds the smaller standard.
No — the larger of the two standards must be applied, not an average.
Yes — but only when both aircraft are turbojets.
No — the smaller standard is always used for efficiency.

items.question_text: Under 8-3-2, for the purpose of longitudinal separation, the terms same track and same course must be considered _____, and crossing tracks and crossing courses must be considered identical.
items.explanation: Per 8-3-2a, same track is considered identical to same course, reciprocal tracks identical to reciprocal courses, and crossing tracks identical to crossing courses.

choices.text: identical

items.question_text: Under 8-3-2, what paragraph defines the course terms referred to in the longitudinal separation rules?
items.explanation: Per the note in 8-3-2, refer to paragraph 1-2-2, Course Definitions.

choices.text: Paragraph 8-3-1, Application.
Paragraph 2-1-29, RVSM Operations.
Paragraph 1-2-2, Course Definitions.
Paragraph 8-4-1, Lateral Separation Application.

items.question_text: Under 8-3-2, how does the longitudinal separation rule for same tracks differ from the rule for crossing tracks?
items.explanation: Per 8-3-2b, same-track separation ensures the estimated spacing between aircraft is never less than the minimum. Crossing-track separation ensures the estimated spacing at the point of intersection is never less than the minimum — the measurement point differs.

choices.text: Same tracks use longitudinal separation while crossing tracks use only vertical separation.
Same tracks use 10-minute minima while crossing tracks use 15-minute minima.
For same tracks, spacing is maintained continuously along the track; for crossing tracks, the minimum is evaluated at the point of intersection.
There is no difference — both use the same measurement point and minimum.

items.question_text: Under 8-3-2, for reciprocal tracks, what two conditions allow discontinuation of vertical separation?
items.explanation: Per 8-3-2b3(b), two conditions: (1) both aircraft have reported passing a significant point and are separated by at least the applicable same-direction longitudinal minimum; or (2) both aircraft have reported passing ground-based NAVAIDs or DME fixes indicating they have passed each other.

choices.text: Immediately after the estimated passing time, OR after one aircraft reports the other in sight.
Both aircraft reported passing a significant point with at least same-direction spacing, OR both reported passed NAVAIDs/DME fixes showing they passed each other.
When both aircraft confirm the same altitude, OR after the following aircraft reports VFR conditions.
After 10 minutes of vertical separation, OR after one aircraft reports a significant point.

items.question_text: Under 8-3-3, Mach number technique applies to _____ aircraft only.
items.explanation: Per 8-3-3a, Mach number technique applies to turbojet aircraft only.

choices.text: turbojet

items.question_text: Under 8-3-3, when the Mach number technique is applied, what is the minimum longitudinal separation when the preceding aircraft maintains a Mach number equal to or greater than the following aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 8-3-3e1(a), 10 minutes provided the preceding aircraft maintains a Mach number equal to or greater than the following aircraft.

choices.text: 5 minutes.
10 minutes.
15 minutes.
9 minutes.

items.question_text: Under 8-3-3, what is the minimum longitudinal separation if the preceding aircraft is Mach 0.06 faster than the following aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 8-3-3e2(e), 5 minutes if the preceding aircraft is Mach 0.06 faster.

items.question_text: Under 8-3-3, what ICAO document is referenced for Mach number technique procedures?
items.explanation: Per the reference block, ICAO DOC 9426-AN/924, Part II, Section 2, is referenced.

choices.text: ICAO DOC 4444, PANS-ATM.
ICAO Annex 11, Air Traffic Services.
ICAO DOC 9426-AN/924, Part II, Section 2.
ICAO DOC 7030, Regional Supplementary Procedures.

items.question_text: Under 8-3-3, what must a pilot do if an immediate temporary Mach number change is essential due to turbulence?
items.explanation: Per the note, turbojet aircraft must request ATC approval before making changes, but if essential due to turbulence, ATC must be notified as soon as possible that the change has been made.

choices.text: Wait for ATC approval before making any change, even in turbulence.
Make the change and report it at the next routine position report.
Deviate from the route instead of changing Mach number.
Notify ATC as soon as possible that the change has been made.

items.question_text: Under 8-3-3, the route requirement for Mach number technique includes same track or continuously diverging tracks. A controller tries to apply Mach number technique to crossing tracks. Is this permissible?
items.explanation: Per 8-3-3b1, routes must follow the same track or continuously diverging tracks. Crossing tracks are not listed as qualifying routes for Mach number technique.

choices.text: No — but reciprocal tracks are permitted.
Yes — crossing tracks qualify if the aircraft have reported over a common point.
Yes — any track configuration qualifies if both aircraft are turbojets.
No — Mach number technique route requirements are same track or continuously diverging tracks only.

items.question_text: Two aircraft on the same track need longitudinal separation. The lead aircraft is a turbojet subject to Mach-number-technique minima; the trailing aircraft is not eligible for those reduced minima. Which longitudinal standard applies?

choices.text: The standard for the trailing aircraft only, because it must follow.
The standard for the lead aircraft only, because it determines the pace.
The larger of the two applicable time-based longitudinal standards, per 8-3-1b.
An equal split between the two standards for fairness.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-1b, when different time-based longitudinal standards apply, the larger of the two standards must be used.

items.situation: You are applying 10-minute longitudinal separation. An automated position report update shows the trailing aircraft is gaining and the actual spacing will drop below 10 minutes before the next fix.
items.para_context: Under 8-3-1, longitudinal separation must ensure estimated positions never drop below the prescribed minimum. If an updated control estimate shows the minimum no longer exists, controllers must reestablish separation and issue traffic information as necessary.

choices.text: Wait for the next pilot position report before taking any action.
Continue applying 10 minutes because the original estimate was valid when separation was established.
Take action to reestablish separation and issue traffic information as necessary.
Switch to distance-based separation from Chapter 6 without issuing traffic information.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-1d, the controller must reestablish separation and issue traffic information when an updated estimate shows the minimum no longer exists.

items.question_text: Two aircraft are on reciprocal tracks. How long must vertical separation be maintained?

choices.text: Only until the aircraft are estimated to pass each other.
For a time interval equal to the applicable minimum required both before and after the aircraft are estimated to pass.
Vertical separation is not required for reciprocal tracks under 8-3-2.
For the entire duration the aircraft are on reciprocal headings regardless of reports.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-2b3(a), reciprocal-track aircraft must be vertically separated for a time interval equal to the applicable minimum before and after they are estimated to pass.

items.situation: Two aircraft on reciprocal tracks are vertically separated. They have both reported passing VORs that are beyond each other's position, confirming they have passed each other. The required before-and-after time interval for vertical separation has elapsed.
items.para_context: Under 8-3-2b3(b), vertical separation may be discontinued after both aircraft have reported passing ground-based NAVAIDs or DME fixes indicating that they have passed each other, or after both have reported passing a significant point and have at least the applicable same-direction longitudinal spacing.

choices.text: Maintain vertical separation until both aircraft land.
Vertical separation may be discontinued because both aircraft have reported passing NAVAID fixes indicating they have passed each other.
Discontinue vertical separation immediately after estimated passing time regardless of reports.
Switch to radar separation before discontinuing vertical.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-2b3(b), vertical separation may be discontinued when both aircraft report passed NAVAID/DME fixes indicating they have passed each other.

items.question_text: Which four conditions must ALL be met before applying Mach number technique reduced longitudinal minima?

choices.text: Any aircraft type; any track configuration; cruise flight only; pilot's discretion for Mach assignment.
Turbojet aircraft only; same track or continuously diverging tracks; aircraft in level, climbing, or descending flight; Mach number assigned to each aircraft.
Turbojet aircraft only; crossing tracks permitted; level flight only; Mach assigned to lead aircraft only.
Turbojet and turboprop; same track only; any altitude; no Mach assignment needed.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-3, all four conditions must be met: turbojet only, qualifying routes, altitude flexibility (level/climbing/descending), and Mach assigned to each aircraft.

items.situation: You want to apply Mach number technique to two turbojet aircraft. Both report over a common point. The lead aircraft is assigned Mach .84, the following aircraft is assigned Mach .82. Both are on the same track and in level flight.
items.para_context: Under 8-3-3, Mach number technique may be applied when the preceding aircraft maintains a Mach number equal to or greater than the following aircraft (minimum 10 minutes), or when the preceding aircraft is faster by specific Mach differentials (5-9 minutes depending on the difference).

choices.text: Apply 12-minute minimum because the lead aircraft is faster by 0.02.
Apply 8-minute minimum because the Mach difference is 0.02.
Apply 10-minute minimum longitudinal separation because the preceding aircraft maintains Mach .84 which is greater than Mach .82, so 10 minutes applies.
Mach number technique cannot be applied because both aircraft must be at exactly the same Mach number.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-3e1(a), when the preceding aircraft maintains a Mach number equal to or greater than the following, the minimum is 10 minutes. Note that the 5-9 minute minima require the preceding aircraft to be faster by at least 0.02-0.06 Mach, but 8-3-3e2's table is a different rule — the 10 minute rule in e1 applies when preceding is equal or greater.

items.instruction: Where would you find the entry-point separation required?
items.question_text: Using the Mach number technique rule-of-thumb, for each 600 NM an aircraft will fly, how many minutes are added for each 0.01 Mach difference when the following aircraft is faster?

choices.text: 1 minute per 300 NM for each 0.01 Mach difference.
2 minutes per 600 NM for each 0.01 Mach difference.
0.5 minutes per 600 NM for each 0.01 Mach difference.
1 minute per 600 NM for each 0.01 Mach difference.

items.explanation: Per the rule-of-thumb in 8-3-3, for each 600 NM, add 1 minute for each 0.01 difference in Mach number. See TBL 8-3-1.
